items.desc:
Fixes #90014

## Problem

`matchArgPattern` in `src/infra/exec-command-resolution.ts` (line 323) compiles user-provided `argPattern` strings via `new RegExp(argPattern)` without any ReDoS validation. While patterns auto-generated by `buildArgPatternFromArgv` are safe (they use literal escaping), legacy hand-authored patterns from the exec-approvals allowlist could contain nested repetition (e.g., `(a+)+$`) that causes catastrophic backtracking.

## Fix

Add a `hasNestedRepetition(argPattern)` gu...

items.title: fix(security): cap state directory writes to 0700
items.desc:
Fixes #89589

## Summary

`writeTextAtomic` computes `dirMode` as `0o777 & ~process.umask()`, which yields `0o775` under the common `umask 0o002`. When the atomic replace helper ensures the parent directory exists, it sets that mode, relaxing a previously hardened `~/.openclaw` from `0700` back to `0775`.

The same applies to `writeJson`, which re-exports from `@openclaw/fs-safe/json` without overriding `dirMode`.

- Defaults `dirMode` to `0o700` in `writeTextAtomic` (was `0o777 & ~pro...

The hardline blocklist in `tools/approval.py` is the unconditional floor that

items.title: fix(security): collapse $IFS whitespace obfuscation before approval checks
items.desc:
## What does this PR do?

Closes a critical bypass of the dangerous-command approval system. The
normalizer that every command passes through before pattern matching
(`_normalize_command_for_detection`) already strips ANSI, null bytes,
fullwidth Unicode, backslash escapes and empty-quote token splits — but
it did nothing about the shell `IFS` variable. In any POSIX shell `$IFS`
and `${IFS}` expand to whitespace, so a command written as
`rm${IFS}-rf${IFS}/` is executed by the live shell as `rm -r...

items.title: fix(security): fail-closed when dangerous-command guard cannot be loaded in TUI shell.exec
items.desc:
## What does this PR do?

`tui_gateway/server.py` exposes a `shell.exec` JSON-RPC method that
runs shell commands via `subprocess.run(shell=True)`. It tries to
block dangerous commands using `tools.approval.detect_dangerous_command`,
but on `ImportError` it silently falls through to executing the command:

```python
# Before (vulnerable — fail-open)
try:
    from tools.approval import detect_dangerous_command
    is_dangerous, _, desc = detect_dangerous_command(cmd)
    if is_dangero...
items.llm_title: hermes-agent命令执行安全守卫加载失败

`ExecTool` with `restrict_to_workspace=True` can read outside the workspace through a relative symlink inside the workspace. The shell guard checks command text and absolute paths, but does not resolve relative symlinks before shell execution.

## Root cause

The workspace restriction for shell commands is syntactic. It rejects some absolute paths and `../`, but relative symlink resolution happens later inside the shell.

## Failure scenario

1. Create `workspace/link.txt -> outside/...

The current implementation allowed any command to execute as long as the absolute paths within the command matched the caller-provided cwd. By setting working_dir to a location outside the workspace, an LLM could manipulate files anywhere on the system it has access to.

items.title: [Security] Wallet signing requires an unforgeable user-authorization channel, not host-resident keys

## Context

PR #3256 adds host-resident credential signers for HMAC, EIP-712, NEP-413, and Solana. The cryptographic implementations are sound — keys never cross the WASM boundary, manifest-bound EIP-712 domains, correct NEP-413 prefix, tight input validation. The architectural problem is independent of the crypto: **the signing key lives in the IronClaw host process and signs silently on every invocation once a tool's capability is approved at install time.**
